title: The Anger Inventory: Naming Every Resentment You're Carrying
dek: *You've been taught that good people don't keep score, but your nervous system has been tallying every unmetabolized hurt since childhood. An anger inventory isn't pettiness, it's precision. It's the practice of naming every resentment you're carrying so your body can finally stop bracing against what it's been holding for you in the dark.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. An anger inventory is a written list of every unspoken resentment you're carrying, from childhood wounds to yesterday's slight. Writing down each grievance moves anger out of your body and onto paper where you can see patterns clearly. This practice doesn't require forgiveness or resolution, just honest naming of what's been festering in silence.

You've been told that keeping track of wrongs makes you bitter. That's convenient advice for people who benefit from your silence. The truth is that your body has been keeping an exquisite record whether you've given yourself permission to or not. Every swallowed objection, every time you smiled through disrespect, every moment you talked yourself out of what you knew, your system logged it. Not because you're petty. Because you're alive. An anger inventory is the deliberate act of writing down every resentment you're aware you're carrying. Not to weaponize them. Not to perform them. To see them. To acknowledge that these things happened, that they mattered, and that pretending they didn't has cost you something. ## What an Anger Inventory Actually Is An anger inventory is a written catalog of unresolved grievances, organized by person, situation, or theme. It's not a journal entry with narrative flow. It's a list. Stark. Specific. Unglamorous. You write the name of the person or situation at the top of a page. Then you list every resentment you can name, one line at a time. "You criticized my weight at Thanksgiving 2019." "You never apologized for forgetting my birthday three years running." "You told me I was too sensitive when I said your joke hurt." Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on shame and anger, notes that many people carry what she calls "invisible resentments," grievances so normalized they've become part of the emotional wallpaper. An inventory makes the invisible visible. It externalizes what's been living as a low-grade hum in your chest. In my own practice, I've watched people cry with relief when they finally write down a resentment they've been carrying for decades. The tears aren't about the original wound. They're about finally being allowed to say it mattered. The format is simple. Each entry follows this shape: "I resent [name] for [specific action] because [impact on me]." The "because" clause is where the power lives. It connects the action to the consequence. "I resent my mother for dismissing my career choice because it taught me my dreams were inconvenient." That's different from "I resent my mother for being unsupportive." Precision matters. ## Why Your Body Needs You to Name It Pat Ogden's work in somatic psychology shows us that unprocessed emotional material doesn't disappear, it gets stored in the body as tension, bracing, and dysregulation. When you swallow anger repeatedly, your nervous system stays in a state of partial activation. Your shoulders rise. Your jaw clenches. Your breath shallows. You're holding a shape around something you won't name. Writing an inventory interrupts this cycle. The act of naming moves the energy from implicit to explicit. It shifts anger from a diffuse bodily state to a concrete cognitive object. You're not trying to solve anything yet. You're just saying: this is here. Research published in PubMed on PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates that chronic anger suppression correlates with increased cardiovascular reactivity. Your body pays a price for silence. An inventory is one way to begin repaying that debt. When you write "I resent my father for mocking my sensitivity," your shoulders might drop half an inch. You might exhale. That's not catharsis. That's your system recognizing that someone finally said the thing it's been holding. You might reach for something like [Gaia Herbs Rhodiola Rosea](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNA2?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support your system through the activation that surfaces when you start naming what's been buried, but the inventory itself is the medicine. ## The Anatomy of a Complete Inventory A complete anger inventory has sections. You're not just listing grievances against one person. You're mapping the full landscape. Start with immediate resentments. These are the recent ones, still hot, from the last six months. They're easy to access because they haven't been buried yet. "I resent my coworker for taking credit for my idea in last Tuesday's meeting." Then move to historical resentments. These are the old ones, sometimes decades old, that still surface when you're tired or triggered. "I resent my high school English teacher for telling me I wasn't college material." These often surprise you with their staying power. Next, categorical resentments. These are patterns, not single incidents. "I resent every romantic partner who has interrupted me mid-sentence." "I resent every family gathering where my boundaries were treated as negotiable." Categories reveal systemic issues. Then, self-resentments. This is where most people balk. "I resent myself for staying in that job two years too long." "I resent myself for not speaking up when I had the chance." Self-directed anger is often the hardest to name because we've been taught it's the same as self-loathing. It's not. It's accountability. Finally, existential resentments. These are the big, formless ones. "I resent that I was born into a body that experiences chronic pain." "I resent that care work is unpaid." These aren't always solvable, but they're real, and they deserve space on the page. Across the articles we've published on this site, we've noticed that people often skip the self-resentment and existential sections. They feel too vulnerable or too abstract. But those sections often hold the keys to understanding why certain interpersonal resentments hit so hard. ## How to Write Your First Inventory Without Drowning Set a timer for twenty minutes. Not longer. This isn't about completion. It's about beginning. Sit somewhere you won't be interrupted. Get a notebook and a pen, not a screen. The hand-to-page connection matters. Your body processes differently when you write by hand. Start with one person. Choose someone who's been on your mind lately, someone whose name brings a little heat to your chest. Write their name at the top of the page. Then list. Don't edit. Don't justify. Don't soften. Just name. "You interrupted me constantly during our last conversation." "You forgot my birthday." "You made a joke about my weight." One line per resentment. If you feel heat rising, your face flushing, your breath quickening, that's normal. You're touching something live. Keep writing. If you need to stop, stop. But notice the urge to stop isn't always about overwhelm. Sometimes it's about loyalty. Sometimes it's about the fear that naming the thing makes you the bad guy. You're not. You're just finally telling the truth. When the timer goes off, put the pen down. You don't have to finish. You don't have to do anything with what you've written. You've just made the invisible visible. That's enough for now. ## What Happens After You Name It Here's what people don't tell you about anger inventories: they change shape over time. What you write today won't be what you write in six months. Some resentments will soften. Others will sharpen. New ones will surface that you didn't have language for yet. An inventory isn't static. It's a living document. You return to it not to rehearse old wounds but to track what's shifting. You might notice that a resentment you thought was about your partner is actually about your mother. You might see that three different people triggered the same wound in three different ways. Harriet Lerner, in her work on the dance of anger, talks about how anger often functions as a signal that something in our relational system is out of balance. An inventory helps you see the pattern beneath the incidents. It's not that five different people are terrible. It's that you have a boundary that keeps getting violated, and you haven't named it yet. In our experience, the real shift happens not when you confront the people on your list but when you see the architecture of your own anger clearly. You realize you're not angry at one person. You're angry at a system. You're angry at a pattern. You're angry at a version of yourself who agreed to things you didn't want. That awareness is what makes change possible. You can't shift a pattern you can't see. Exploring [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) can give you practical ways to metabolize what surfaces once you've named it. ## The Practice: A Five-Minute Inventory Snapshot You don't need an hour. You need five minutes and a willingness to name one thing. Sit down with a blank page. At the top, write: "Right now, I'm carrying resentment toward..." Set a timer for five minutes. Write without stopping. Stream of consciousness. No punctuation required. No complete sentences required. Just names and fragments. "Boss. Meeting. Interrupted. Didn't listen. Sister. Borrowed money. Never paid back. Ex. Lied about." When the timer goes off, read what you wrote once. Don't analyze it. Just notice what's there. Notice what surprised you. Notice what you already knew but haven't said out loud. That's the practice. Five minutes. One page. A snapshot of what you're holding today. You might feel lighter. You might feel heavier. Both are fine. You've just done something most people never do: you've told the truth about what you're carrying. ## When Naming Isn't Enough Sometimes you write the inventory and nothing shifts. The resentments are still there, as solid as ever. You've named them, you've seen them, and they haven't moved. That's when you know it's time for the next step. Naming is necessary, but it's not always sufficient. Some anger needs to be spoken, not just written. Some anger needs to be moved through your body, not just cataloged on a page. Some anger needs a witness, not just a journal. If you're feeling stuck after an inventory, consider working with a somatic therapist who understands that anger lives in tissue, not just thought. Consider voice work, where you practice saying the unsayable out loud in a safe container. Consider movement practices that let your body discharge what it's been holding. A support like [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Vervain - Relax & Calm - Natural Homeopathic Flow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000MXAHDU?tag=spankyspinola-20) might help soften the edges of the activation that comes up when you're in the thick of this work. And sometimes [Garden of Life Organics B-12 Spray (Methylcobalamin)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00K5NEPJY?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your nervous system when you're processing old material that's been metabolically expensive to hold. But the inventory remains foundational. It's the map. Everything else is the journey. ## Common Questions About Anger Inventories ### What if I start writing and can't stop? That's a sign you've been holding more than you realized. Let yourself write. Set a longer timer if you need to. Ninety minutes. Two hours. Let the flood come. Your system has been waiting for permission. When you're done, put the pages somewhere safe. You don't have to reread them immediately. You've already done the work by writing them. ### Do I have to share my inventory with anyone? No. This is for you first. Some people eventually share pieces of their inventory with a therapist or trusted friend, but that's a later step. Right now, the practice is about honoring your own experience. Sharing prematurely can turn the inventory into a performance or an argument. Keep it private until you're clear about what you need from it. ### What if writing the inventory makes me angrier? That's not the inventory making you angrier. That's you finally feeling the anger that was already there. Suppressed anger doesn't disappear, it goes underground. When you name it, it surfaces. That can feel like an increase, but it's actually just visibility. The anger was always this big. You're just finally seeing it. Let yourself feel it. It will move if you don't block it. --- The inventory you write today won't be complete. It can't be. You're a living person with new experiences every day, and some of those experiences will generate new resentments. Some old ones will fade. Some will calcify. But starting the practice, naming even one thing you've been carrying in silence, that changes the relationship you have with your own anger. You're no longer pretending. You're no longer performing ease you don't feel. You're telling the truth, even if only to yourself, even if only on a page no one else will see. That truth is a kind of medicine. It won't fix everything, but it will stop the slow leak of energy that comes from holding things you won't name. You might find yourself drawn to [more articles in this journal](/articles) or the companion practices at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), but the inventory itself is the threshold. It's where you stop betraying yourself in the name of keeping the peace. You can also explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for supports that work alongside this emotional archaeology, but no herb will do the naming for you. That's your work. That's your power. And once you've named it, you'll know what to do next. Not because someone told you. Because your body, finally unburdened of the weight of pretending, will show you. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on the anger inventory: naming every resentment you're carrying, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
